text: The surgeon begins the procedure by incising the peritoneum lateral to the left colon, identifying and mobilizing the left ureter. The ureter is divided at the intramural tunnel, with periureteral tissue preserved. A hemolock clip is applied at the bladder insertion, and the proximal ureter is clipped with a pre-tied suture attached to a hemolock clip. The ureter is mobilized across the iliac vessels for sufficient length to cross to the right side for urinary diversion. A similar approach is used to isolate the right ureter.

The surgeon develops the pre-rectal and posterior vesicle space by identifying the pouch of Douglas and incising the anterior peritoneum along the posterior bladder surface near the seminal vesicles. Denonvilliers' fascia is incised, and dissection proceeds in the posterior plane along the rectum's longitudinal fibers, separating it from the bladder and prostate. The dissection extends caudally to the prostate apex. A left lymphadenectomy is performed using a split and roll technique, with an extended pelvic lymph dissection between the external and internal iliac vessels, including external iliac, pre-sacral, common iliac, paracaval, and para-aortic nodes. The contralateral nodal dissection is conducted similarly.

The surgeon visualizes and divides the bladder and prostate pedicles bilaterally using a ligature. Dissection proceeds to the endopelvic fascia, which is incised bilaterally to expose the prostate and its apex. The space of Retzius is developed, with the superficial venous complex cauterized and the deep venous complex secured with suture. The deep venous complex is incised proximal to the suture, and the urethra is isolated and transected, revealing the Foley catheter.

The surgeon clips and divides the Foley catheter, then rotates the specimen with the catheter pointing anteriorly to prevent urine spillage. Attention is turned to creating an ileal conduit, with the left ureter passed behind the mesentery of the sigmoid colon. The peritoneum overlying the sigmoid mesentery is incised if necessary, and the right side grasper gently advances through the mesentery. The left ureter is grasped and passed through the mesenteric window.

The surgeon identifies the terminal ileum in the right abdomen and selects a healthy segment approximately fifteen centimeters from the ileocecal junction. The ileum is stretched from the retroperitoneum to the abdominal wall to estimate the length. A sixty millimeter endovascular stapler is used to divide the ileal segment and attached mesentery, ensuring the stapler is placed perpendicular to the anti-mesenteric border to prevent devascularization and ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon uses scissors to open the anti-mesenteric ends of the bowel while keeping the staple lines intact. The bowel anastomosis is created by aligning the anti-mesenteric portions and positioning the stapler into the bowel end, re-establishing continuity in a side-to-side fashion. After firing the stapler, the integrity and length of the anastomosis are assessed before closing it with a transverse load from the stapler. The anastomosis is completed with additional sixty millimeter staples, and the crotch is reinforced with a three-oh silk suture. The mesenteric window is closed.

The surgeon tacks the ureters to the pelvic side wall and spatulates their distal ends for approximately 2.5 centimeters. Using a running 4-0 PDS suture, the ureters are anastomosed with the Wallace technique. The back wall of the ureters is secured to the butt of the ileal conduit. Seven French urinary diversion stents are placed over a 3/8 guide wire into the ureters. A 20 French council tip catheter is positioned in the pelvis, and the stents are passed through the ileal conduit. The distal staple line of the conduit is excised, and the unused portion of the distal ureter is removed.

The surgeon completes the uretero-ileal anastomosis and retroperitonealizes the ileal conduit using interrupted II-O silk sutures. IV-O chromic sutures anchor the stents to the ileal conduit. Dissection proceeds through the fascia between the rectus muscle into the abdominal cavity, and the distal end of the ileal conduit and stents are grasped with a Babcock clamp and brought through the stomal site. The robot is undocked, and stomal creation is performed in a standard rosebud fashion. A small fan and steel incision is made to extract the intact bladder and prostate specimen.
